Territories controlled by the [Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham](/wiki/Hay%27at_Tahrir_al-Sham "Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham") (white) and [SOR](/wiki/Southern_Operations_Room "Southern Operations Room") (pink), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [SNA](/wiki/Syrian_National_Army "Syrian National Army") (light green), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [SDF](/wiki/Syrian_Democratic_Forces "Syrian Democratic Forces") (yellow), [IS](/wiki/Islamic_State "Islamic State") (grey), the [Syrian uncertain/mixed](/wiki/Syrian "Syrian") (red/light grey), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [SFA](/wiki/Revolutionary_Commando_Army "Revolutionary Commando Army") (teal), [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_Syria_(2024%E2%80%93present) "Israeli invasion of Syria (2024–present)") (blue).

Rebel forces reached the outskirts of [Homs](/wiki/Homs "Homs") on 5 December, beginning a [three-day battle](/wiki/2024_Homs_offensive "2024 Homs offensive") for the city. Simultaneously, an HTS-coordinated[[148]](#cite_note-159)[[149]](#cite_note-:32-160) [mass uprising](/wiki/Southern_Syria_offensive_(2024) "Southern Syria offensive (2024)") led by a [coalition of Druze tribes and opposition forces](/wiki/Southern_Operations_Room "Southern Operations Room") captured the southern cities of [Sweida](/wiki/Sweida "Sweida") and [Daraa](/wiki/Daraa_insurgency "Daraa insurgency") by 6 December,[[150]](#cite_note-161) and rapidly advanced northwards to encircle Damascus over the following day.[[151]](#cite_note-reuters-homs-162)[[152]](#cite_note-163) Homs was captured by rebel forces by the early morning of 8 December, leaving no major regime strongholds between the rebel advance and Damascus itself.[[153]](#cite_note-Captured-164)

Cut off from the [Alawite](/wiki/Alawites "Alawites") heartland of [Tartus](/wiki/Tartus_Governorate "Tartus Governorate") and [Latakia](/wiki/Latakia_Governorate "Latakia Governorate") governorates, faced with a rebel pincer from both north and south bearing down on Damascus, and with no hope of foreign intervention from the regime's Russian and Iranian benefactors, Assadist authority over remaining regime-held territories rapidly disintegrated.[[154]](#cite_note-165)[[155]](#cite_note-reuters-166) The [Syrian Arab Armed Forces](/wiki/Syrian_Arab_Armed_Forces "Syrian Arab Armed Forces") melted away as its soldiers abandoned their weapons and uniforms,[[156]](#cite_note-guard2024-167) many [deserting](/wiki/Desertion "Desertion") across the border to [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq") and [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"). Opposition forces [captured](/wiki/Fall_of_Damascus_(2024) "Fall of Damascus (2024)") the capital Damascus on 8 December, toppling Bashar al-Assad's government and ending the Assad family's 53-year-long rule over the country.[[151]](#cite_note-reuters-homs-162) Assad fled to [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") with his family, where he was granted asylum.[[157]](#cite_note-168)[[158]](#cite_note-169)

### Post-Ba'athist and transitional period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Syrian_rebels_in_Aleppo%2C_30_November_2024.png/250px-Syrian_rebels_in_Aleppo%2C_30_November_2024.png)](/wiki/File:Syrian_rebels_in_Aleppo,_30_November_2024.png)

Syrian revolutionaries toppling a statue of [Bassel al-Assad](/wiki/Bassel_al-Assad "Bassel al-Assad") in [New Aleppo](/wiki/New_Aleppo "New Aleppo"), 30 November 2024

Following the [fall of the Assad regime](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Assad_regime "Fall of the Assad regime"), Assad's ninth prime minister [Mohammad Ghazi al-Jalali](/wiki/Mohammad_Ghazi_al-Jalali "Mohammad Ghazi al-Jalali"), with support from the opposition and [Ahmed al-Sharaa](/wiki/Ahmed_al-Sharaa "Ahmed al-Sharaa"), remained at his post in a caretaker capacity until a [caretaker government](/wiki/Syrian_caretaker_government "Syrian caretaker government") led by [Mohammed al-Bashir](/wiki/Mohammed_al-Bashir "Mohammed al-Bashir") was formed the following day.[[159]](#cite_note-170)[[160]](#cite_note-ilkha.com-171) Al-Jalali called for fresh elections so that the Syrian people may choose their new leaders.[[161]](#cite_note-172)[[151]](#cite_note-reuters-homs-162)

Prior to the fall of the Assad regime, Mohammed al-Bashir headed the [Syrian Salvation Government](/wiki/Syrian_Salvation_Government "Syrian Salvation Government") (SSG) formed in the province of [Idlib](/wiki/Idlib "Idlib") by [Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham](/wiki/Hay%27at_Tahrir_al-Sham "Hay'at Tahrir al-Sham") (HTS), the Islamist militant organization which led the overthrow of Assad in December 2024. In general, the formation of the Transitional Government was scaling of the SSG "to the whole of Syria", as the composition of the new government was almost the same as that of the SSG. According to a report by the Syrian Network for Human Rights, critics and opponents of the HTS were subject to repression in forms of enforced disappearances and tortures.[[162]](#cite_note-173) Shortly after the fall of the Assad regime, [Israel commenced a ground invasion](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_Syria_(2024%E2%80%93present) "Israeli invasion of Syria (2024–present)") of the [Purple Line](/wiki/Purple_Line_(ceasefire_line) "Purple Line (ceasefire line)") buffer zone near the Golan Heights, as well as commencing a series of airstrikes against Syrian military depots and naval bases.[[163]](#cite_note-NYT_12-8-174)[[164]](#cite_note-175) The [Israeli Defense Forces](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces") claims that it is destroying Ba'athist military infrastructure, including chemical weapons plants, so that the rebels cannot use them.[[163]](#cite_note-NYT_12-8-174)

Despite the collapse of the Assad regime, Turkish-backed [Syrian National Army](/wiki/Syrian_National_Army "Syrian National Army") fighters in northern Syria continued their [offensive](/wiki/Manbij_offensive_(2024) "Manbij offensive (2024)") against U.S.-backed [Syrian Democratic Forces](/wiki/Syrian_Democratic_Forces "Syrian Democratic Forces") (SDF) forces until a ceasefire was reached on 11 December.[[165]](#cite_note-176)[[166]](#cite_note-177) In February 2025, the SDF, the [Autonomous Administration](/wiki/Democratic_Autonomous_Administration_of_North_and_East_Syria "Democratic Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria"), and the [Syrian Democratic Council](/wiki/Syrian_Democratic_Council "Syrian Democratic Council") decided in a meeting that SDF would merge with the [Syrian Armed Forces](/wiki/Syrian_Armed_Forces "Syrian Armed Forces"). The [International Coalition against ISIS](/wiki/Operation_Inherent_Resolve "Operation Inherent Resolve") voiced support for continued dialogue between the SDF and the new Syrian government as well.[[167]](#cite_note-178)

The prime minister of the transitional government, Mohammed al-Bashir, has promised to allow Christians and other minorities to continue practicing their religion without interference. However, this has been met with doubts as many rebel forces had previous connections to [al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda") and the [Islamic State](/wiki/Islamic_State "Islamic State").[[168]](#cite_note-179)[[169]](#cite_note-180)[[170]](#cite_note-181) The use of a variation of the [*Tawhid* flag](/wiki/Islamic_flag "Islamic flag") by the new government alongside [the opposition flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Syria "Flag of Syria") also raised worries, as it implies that the new state may be [less secular](/wiki/Islam_and_secularism "Islam and secularism").[[171]](#cite_note-flag1-182)[[172]](#cite_note-flag2-183) [Aisha al-Dibs](/wiki/Aisha_al-Dibs "Aisha al-Dibs") was appointed as the Minister of Women's Affairs on 22 December 2024.[[173]](#cite_note-184)
On 12 December 2024, a spokesman of the transitional government speaking to [Agence France-Presse](/wiki/Agence_France-Presse "Agence France-Presse") said that during the government's three-month term, the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Syria "Constitution of Syria") and [parliament](/wiki/People%27s_Assembly_of_Syria "People's Assembly of Syria") would be suspended and that a 'judicial and human rights committee' would be established to review the constitution, prior to making amendments.[[174]](#cite_note-185)

On 29 January 2025, during the [Syrian Revolution Victory Conference](/wiki/Syrian_Revolution_Victory_Conference "Syrian Revolution Victory Conference") in [Presidential Palace](/wiki/People%27s_Palace_of_Syria "People's Palace of Syria"), the Syrian General Command appointed [Ahmed al-Sharaa](/wiki/Ahmed_al-Sharaa "Ahmed al-Sharaa") as president for the transitional period after he had served as the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* leader following the fall of the Assad regime.[[175]](#cite_note-english.enabbaladi.net-186)

In March 2025, the UK-based [SOHR](/wiki/Syrian_Observatory_for_Human_Rights "Syrian Observatory for Human Rights") reported that Syrian security forces and pro-government fighters had committed a [massacre of more than 1500 Alawite civilians](/wiki/2025_massacres_of_Syrian_Alawites "2025 massacres of Syrian Alawites") during [clashes in western Syria](/wiki/March_2025_Western_Syria_clashes "March 2025 Western Syria clashes").[[176]](#cite_note-187)[[177]](#cite_note-188)

On 10 March 2025, the SDF agreed to merge with the Syrian Armed Forces after SDF leader [Mazloum Abdi](/wiki/Mazloum_Abdi "Mazloum Abdi") met with al-Sharaa.[[178]](#cite_note-189) Three days later, al-Sharaa signed an [interim constitution](/wiki/2025_Interim_Constitution_of_Syria "2025 Interim Constitution of Syria") covering a five-year transitional period.[[179]](#cite_note-190)

On 29 March 2025, the [Syrian transitional government](/wiki/Syrian_transitional_government "Syrian transitional government") was announced by Syrian president [Ahmed al-Sharaa](/wiki/Ahmed_al-Sharaa "Ahmed al-Sharaa") at a ceremony at the [Presidential Palace](/wiki/People%27s_Palace_of_Syria "People's Palace of Syria") in [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus"),[[180]](#cite_note-:022-191) in which the new ministers were sworn in and delivered speeches outlining their agendas.[[181]](#cite_note-syria-tv-2025-03-29-192) The government replaced the [Syrian caretaker government](/wiki/Syrian_caretaker_government "Syrian caretaker government"), which was formed following the fall of the Assad regime on 8 December 2024.[[182]](#cite_note-auto3-193) Since then, a number of Western governments have lifted the sanctions on Syria, most notably the United States and the United Kingdom.[[183]](#cite_note-194)[[184]](#cite_note-195)

Between 6 and 11 January 2026, [fighting](/wiki/2026_northeastern_Syria_offensive "2026 northeastern Syria offensive") in Aleppo between Syrian government forces and the Kurdish-led [Syrian Democratic Forces](/wiki/Syrian_Democratic_Forces "Syrian Democratic Forces") had resulted in the deaths of 23 people and displaced 148,000 residents.[[185]](#cite_note-196)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Syria](/wiki/Geography_of_Syria "Geography of Syria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Water_Stress%2C_Top_Countries_%282020%29.svg/250px-Water_Stress%2C_Top_Countries_%282020%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Water_Stress,_Top_Countries_(2020).svg)

Syria is the twelfth most water-stressed country in the world.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Deir_Ez-Zor_Euphrates_9929.jpg/250px-Deir_Ez-Zor_Euphrates_9929.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deir_Ez-Zor_Euphrates_9929.jpg)

The Euphrates is the longest and one of the oldest rivers in [Western Asia](/wiki/Western_Asia "Western Asia")

Syria's climate varies from the humid Mediterranean coast, through a semi-arid steppe zone, to arid desert in the east. The country consists mostly of arid plateau, although the northwest part bordering the Mediterranean is fairly green. [Al-Jazira](/wiki/Upper_Mesopotamia "Upper Mesopotamia") in the northeast and [Hauran](/wiki/Hauran "Hauran") in the south are important agricultural areas. The [Euphrates](/wiki/Euphrates "Euphrates"), Syria's most important river, crosses the country in the east. Syria is one of the fifteen states that comprise the so-called "[cradle of civilization](/wiki/Cradle_of_civilization "Cradle of civilization")".[[186]](#cite_note-197) Its land straddles the northwest of the [Arabian plate](/wiki/Arabian_plate "Arabian plate").[[187]](#cite_note-198)

Petroleum in commercial quantities was first discovered in the northeast in 1956. The most important oil fields are those of al-Suwaydiyah, Karatchok, [Rmelan](/wiki/Rmelan "Rmelan") near [al-Hasakah](/wiki/Al-Hasakah "Al-Hasakah"), as well as [al-Omar](/wiki/Al-Omar_field "Al-Omar field") and al-Taym fields near [Deir ez-Zor](/wiki/Deir_ez-Zor "Deir ez-Zor"). The fields are a natural extension of the Iraqi fields of [Mosul](/wiki/Mosul "Mosul") and [Kirkuk](/wiki/Kirkuk "Kirkuk"). Petroleum became Syria's leading natural resource and chief export after 1974. Natural gas was discovered at the field of Jbessa in 1940.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Wildlife of Syria](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Syria "Wildlife of Syria")

See also: [Environmental issues in Syria](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Syria "Environmental issues in Syria")

Syria contains four terrestrial ecoregions: [Middle East steppe](/wiki/Middle_East_steppe "Middle East steppe"), [Eastern Mediterranean conifer–sclerophyllous–broadleaf forests](/wiki/Eastern_Mediterranean_conifer%E2%80%93sclerophyllous%E2%80%93broadleaf_forests "Eastern Mediterranean conifer–sclerophyllous–broadleaf forests"), [Southern Anatolian montane conifer and deciduous forests](/wiki/Southern_Anatolian_montane_conifer_and_deciduous_forests "Southern Anatolian montane conifer and deciduous forests"), and [Mesopotamian shrub desert](/wiki/Mesopotamian_shrub_desert "Mesopotamian shrub desert").[[188]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-199) The country had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 3.64/10, ranking it 144th globally out of 172 countries.[[189]](#cite_note-FLII-Supplementary-200)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Government of Syria](/wiki/Government_of_Syria "Government of Syria")

See also: [Elections in Syria](/wiki/Elections_in_Syria "Elections in Syria")

### Post-Ba'athist Syria (2024–present)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Ahmed_al-Sharaa_in_November_2025.jpg/250px-Ahmed_al-Sharaa_in_November_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ahmed_al-Sharaa_in_November_2025.jpg)

[Ahmed al-Sharaa](/wiki/Ahmed_al-Sharaa "Ahmed al-Sharaa")  
[President](/wiki/President_of_Syria "President of Syria")

Syria is currently undergoing a political transition following the [fall of the Assad regime](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Assad_regime "Fall of the Assad regime") on 8 December 2024.[[190]](#cite_note-201) A [caretaker government](/wiki/Syrian_caretaker_government "Syrian caretaker government"), led by [Mohammed al-Bashir](/wiki/Mohammed_al-Bashir "Mohammed al-Bashir") was then formed to govern the country.[[191]](#cite_note-202)[[192]](#cite_note-203) On 29 January 2025, during the [Syrian Revolution Victory Conference](/wiki/Syrian_Revolution_Victory_Conference "Syrian Revolution Victory Conference") in the [Presidential Palace](/wiki/Presidential_Palace,_Damascus "Presidential Palace, Damascus"), the Syrian General Command appointed [Ahmed al-Sharaa](/wiki/Ahmed_al-Sharaa "Ahmed al-Sharaa") as president for the transitional period after he had served as the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* leader following the fall of the Assad regime.[[175]](#cite_note-english.enabbaladi.net-186)

On 13 March 2025, al-Sharaa ratified the [interim constitution](/wiki/2025_Interim_Constitution_of_Syria "2025 Interim Constitution of Syria"), which will be valid for five years.[[193]](#cite_note-CNN20250313-204)[[194]](#cite_note-WP20250313-205) The constitution sets a [presidential system](/wiki/Presidential_system "Presidential system"),[[195]](#cite_note-206) without the position of [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Syria "Prime Minister of Syria").[[196]](#cite_note-207) The [People's Assembly](/wiki/People%27s_Assembly_of_Syria "People's Assembly of Syria") was also established to serve as an interim parliament during the five-year transition, overseeing the drafting of a new permanent constitution.[[197]](#cite_note-:02-208) On 29 March 2025, the [Syrian transitional government](/wiki/Syrian_transitional_government "Syrian transitional government") was announced by al-Sharaa at a ceremony at the [Presidential Palace](/wiki/Presidential_Palace,_Damascus "Presidential Palace, Damascus") in Damascus,[[198]](#cite_note-:023-209) in which the new ministers were sworn in and delivered speeches outlining their agendas.[[181]](#cite_note-syria-tv-2025-03-29-192) The government replaced the [Syrian caretaker government](/wiki/Syrian_caretaker_government "Syrian caretaker government"), which was formed following the fall of the Assad regime on 8 December 2024.[[182]](#cite_note-auto3-193)

Currently, Syria operates under a unitary system of government[[199]](#cite_note-210) but there are proposals for a [implementation of decentralisation](/wiki/Federalization_of_Syria "Federalization of Syria").

### Ba'athist Syria (1963–2024)

Main articles: [Ba'athist Syria](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Syria "Ba'athist Syria") and [Politics of Ba'athist Syria](/wiki/Politics_of_Ba%27athist_Syria "Politics of Ba'athist Syria")

The Syrian Arab Republic was a [presidential state](/wiki/Presidential_republic "Presidential republic")[[200]](#cite_note-211) that nominally permitted the candidacy of individuals who were not part of the Ba'ath-controlled [National Progressive Front](/wiki/National_Progressive_Front_(Syria) "National Progressive Front (Syria)").[[201]](#cite_note-212)[[202]](#cite_note-213) Despite this, Syria remained a one-party state with an extensive [secret police](/wiki/General_Intelligence_Directorate_(Syria) "General Intelligence Directorate (Syria)") apparatus that curtailed any independent political activity.[[203]](#cite_note-auto1-214)[[204]](#cite_note-215) The constitution introduced unilaterally by the Assad regime, without the participation of the [Syrian opposition](/wiki/Syrian_opposition "Syrian opposition"), had bolstered its authoritarian character by bestowing extraordinary powers on the presidency, and a Ba'athist political committee continued to be responsible for authorization of political parties.[[205]](#cite_note-216)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Ali_Khamenei_meets_Bashar_al-Assad_in_Tehran_20190225_03.jpg/250px-Ali_Khamenei_meets_Bashar_al-Assad_in_Tehran_20190225_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ali_Khamenei_meets_Bashar_al-Assad_in_Tehran_20190225_03.jpg)

Syrian president [Bashar al-Assad](/wiki/Bashar_al-Assad "Bashar al-Assad") with Ayatollah [Ali Khamenei](/wiki/Ali_Khamenei "Ali Khamenei") in Tehran, Iran, 25 February 2019

The ruling Arab Socialist Ba'ath Party governed Syria as a [police state](/wiki/Police_state "Police state") through its control of the [Syrian military](/wiki/Syrian_Arab_Armed_Forces "Syrian Arab Armed Forces") and security apparatus;[[206]](#cite_note-217) this system has frequently been described as [totalitarian](/wiki/Totalitarian "Totalitarian"),[[h]](#cite_note-tots-97) although some scholars have preferred the term [authoritarian](/wiki/Authoritarian "Authoritarian").[[90]](#cite_note-nt1-98)[[91]](#cite_note-nt2-99)[[88]](#cite_note-Heydemann,_Steven_19992-95) The 50th edition of [Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World"), published by [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") in 2023, designated Syria as "Worst of the Worst" among the "Not Free" countries and gave it the lowest score (1/100) alongside [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan").[[203]](#cite_note-auto1-214)[[207]](#cite_note-218)

According to the [2012 Syrian constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Syria#Constitution_of_2012 "Constitution of Syria"), the [President of Syria](/wiki/President_of_Syria "President of Syria") was the head of the Syrian state, while the [Prime Minister of Syria](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Syria "Prime Minister of Syria") was nominally the head of government,[[208]](#cite_note-con-2012-219)[*[unreliable source?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources")*] although real power in the system lay with the presidency.[[209]](#cite_note-220) The legislature, the [People's Assembly](/wiki/People%27s_Assembly_of_Syria "People's Assembly of Syria"), was the body responsible for passing laws, approving government [appropriations](/wiki/Appropriation_(law) "Appropriation (law)") and debating policy.[[208]](#cite_note-con-2012-219) In the event of a [vote of no confidence](/wiki/Vote_of_no_confidence "Vote of no confidence") by a simple majority, the prime minister was required to tender the resignation of their government to the president.[[208]](#cite_note-con-2012-219) Since the rule of Hafez al-Assad, the Ba'athist political system was centered around a comprehensive [cult of personality](/wiki/Al-Assad_family#Cult_of_Personality "Al-Assad family") focused on the Assad family;[[210]](#cite_note-221)[[211]](#cite_note-222)[[212]](#cite_note-223)[[213]](#cite_note-224) with Alawite loyalists of the Ba'ath party dominating key positions in the military apparatus, secret police, and political establishment.[[100]](#cite_note-The_Sturdy_House_That_Assad_Built2-108)

The executive branch consisted of the president, two [vice presidents](/wiki/Vice_President_of_Syria "Vice President of Syria"), the prime minister, and the [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_of_Syria "Council of Ministers of Syria") (cabinet). The constitution required the president to be a Muslim but did not make Islam the state religion.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86)[[214]](#cite_note-const-225) On 31 January 1973, Hafez al-Assad implemented a new constitution, which led to a national crisis. Unlike previous constitutions, this one did not require that the President of Syria be a Muslim, leading to fierce demonstrations in Hama, Homs, and Aleppo organized by the [Muslim Brotherhood](/wiki/Muslim_Brotherhood "Muslim Brotherhood") and the traditional *[ulama](/wiki/Ulama "Ulama")*. They labelled Assad the "enemy of [Allah](/wiki/Allah "Allah")" and called for a *[jihad](/wiki/Jihad "Jihad")* against his rule.[[215]](#cite_note-226) The government survived a series of [armed revolts](/wiki/Islamist_uprising_in_Syria "Islamist uprising in Syria") led mostly by [Islamists](/wiki/Islamist "Islamist") of the [Muslim Brotherhood](/wiki/Muslim_Brotherhood "Muslim Brotherhood"), between 1976 and 1982, through a series of repressions and massacres. The constitution gave the president the right to appoint ministers, to declare war and [state of emergency](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency"), to issue laws (which, except in the case of emergency, require ratification by the People's Council), to declare amnesty, to amend the constitution, and to appoint civil servants and military personnel.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86) According to the 2012 constitution, the president was elected by Syrian citizens in a direct election. Syria's legislative branch was the unicameral People's Council. The People's Council primarily served as an institution to validate Syria's one-party system and re-affirm the legislative proceedings of the Arab Socialist Ba'ath party.[[216]](#cite_note-227)

There was no independent judiciary in Syria, since all judges and prosecutors were required to be Ba'athist appointees.[[203]](#cite_note-auto1-214) Syria's judicial branches include the [Supreme Constitutional Court](/wiki/Supreme_Constitutional_Court_of_Syria "Supreme Constitutional Court of Syria"), the [High Judicial Council](/wiki/High_Judicial_Council "High Judicial Council"), the Court of Cassation, and the [State Security](/wiki/Security_agency "Security agency") Courts. [Islamic](/wiki/Islamic "Islamic") jurisprudence was a main source of legislation and Syria's judicial system had elements of [Ottoman](/wiki/Mecelle "Mecelle"), [French](/wiki/French_law "French law"), and [Islamic](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") laws. Syria had three levels of courts: courts of first instance, courts of appeals, and the constitutional court, the highest [tribunal](/wiki/Tribunal "Tribunal"). Religious courts handled questions of personal and family law.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86) The Supreme State Security Court was abolished by Bashar al-Assad in 2011.[[217]](#cite_note-decree53-228) As a result of the ongoing civil war, various alternative governments were formed, including the [Syrian Interim Government](/wiki/Syrian_Interim_Government "Syrian Interim Government"), the [Democratic Union Party](/wiki/Democratic_Union_Party_(Syria) "Democratic Union Party (Syria)") and localized regions governed by sharia. Representatives of the Syrian Interim government were invited to take up Syria's seat at the Arab League in 2013 and[[218]](#cite_note-guardian.co.uk-229) was recognised as the "sole representative of the Syrian people" by several nations including the United States, United Kingdom, and France.[[219]](#cite_note-bbc.co.uk-230)[[220]](#cite_note-bbc-231)[[221]](#cite_note-usatoday.com-232)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Bab_Dreeb_Demonstration%2C_Homs.jpg/250px-Bab_Dreeb_Demonstration%2C_Homs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bab_Dreeb_Demonstration,_Homs.jpg)

[Protest against the Assad regime](/wiki/Syrian_revolution "Syrian revolution") in the city of [Homs](/wiki/Homs "Homs"), 3 February 2012

[Syria's elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Syria "Elections in Syria") were conducted through a [sham process](/wiki/Sham_election "Sham election"), characterised by wide-scale [rigging](/wiki/Rigging_election "Rigging election"), repetitive voting and absence of [voter registration](/wiki/Voter_registration "Voter registration") and verification systems.[[222]](#cite_note-233)[[223]](#cite_note-234)[[224]](#cite_note-auto2-235) Parliamentary elections were held on 13 April 2016 in the government-controlled areas of Syria, for all 250 seats of Syria's unicameral legislature, the Majlis al-Sha'ab, or the People's Council of Syria.[[225]](#cite_note-236) Even before results had been announced, several nations, including Germany, the United States and the United Kingdom, have declared their refusal to accept the results, largely citing it "not representing the will of the Syrian people."[[226]](#cite_note-237) However, representatives of the Russian Federation have voiced their support of this election's results. Various independent observers and international organizations have denounced the Assad regime's electoral conduct as a scam; with the United Nations condemning it as illegitimate elections with "no mandate".[[227]](#cite_note-238)[[228]](#cite_note-239)[[229]](#cite_note-240)[[224]](#cite_note-auto2-235) [Electoral Integrity Project](/wiki/Electoral_Integrity_Project "Electoral Integrity Project")'s 2022 Global report designated Syrian elections as a "facade" with the worst electoral integrity in the world alongside [Comoros](/wiki/Comoros "Comoros") and [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic").[[230]](#cite_note-241)[[231]](#cite_note-242) Three alternative governments formed during the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war"), the [Syrian Interim Government](/wiki/Syrian_Interim_Government "Syrian Interim Government") (formed in 2013), [Rojava](/wiki/Autonomous_Administration_of_North_and_East_Syria "Autonomous Administration of North and East Syria") (formed in 2016) and the [Syrian Salvation Government](/wiki/Syrian_Salvation_Government "Syrian Salvation Government") (formed in 2017), controlled northern areas of the country and operated independently of the Syrian Arab Republic.

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Syria](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Syria "Foreign relations of Syria")

#### Post-Ba'athist Syria

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Diplomatic_relations_of_Syria.svg/330px-Diplomatic_relations_of_Syria.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Diplomatic_relations_of_Syria.svg)

[Diplomatic relations of Syria](/wiki/List_of_diplomatic_missions_of_Syria "List of diplomatic missions of Syria")

After the [fall of the Assad regime](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Assad_regime "Fall of the Assad regime") and the subsequent formation of the [Syrian transitional government](/wiki/Syrian_transitional_government "Syrian transitional government"), Syria has begun to re-establish diplomatic ties with nations that previously broke ties with the Ba'athist-led Syrian government during the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war"), alongside the easing and removal of economic sanctions previously placed onto the Ba'athist government, including the [Caesar Act](/wiki/Caesar_Syria_Civilian_Protection_Act "Caesar Syria Civilian Protection Act") by the United States.

The Syrian transitional government had also changed its stance on returning the Golan Heights to demand the [withdrawal of Israel](/wiki/Israeli_invasion_of_Syria_(2024%E2%80%93present) "Israeli invasion of Syria (2024–present)") from areas beyond the border agreed upon in the 1974 Disengagement Agreement.

The United States has also announced plans to re-open its embassy in Damascus, which was closed during the Syrian civil war on February 21, 2026, which would take 15 days according to the [United States Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State").[[232]](#cite_note-243) Prior, [Tom Barrack](/wiki/Tom_Barrack "Tom Barrack"), the United States special envoy to Syria, had risen the American flag at the ambassador's residence on May 2025.

The Syrian transitional government had also restored diplomatic ties with [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), with the latter now helping the former in the restructuring and training of the [Syrian Armed Forces](/wiki/Syrian_Armed_Forces "Syrian Armed Forces") alongside assisting the Syrian military during the [SDF–Syrian transitional government clashes](/wiki/SDF%E2%80%93Syrian_transitional_government_clashes_(2025%E2%80%93present) "SDF–Syrian transitional government clashes (2025–present)") in the form of military aid.

On March 7, 2026, following the lifting of Canadian economic sanctions on Syria, the [Bank of Canada](/wiki/Bank_of_Canada "Bank of Canada") agreed to open an account in the [Central Bank of Syria](/wiki/Central_Bank_of_Syria "Central Bank of Syria").[[233]](#cite_note-244)

#### Ba'athist Syria

Ensuring national security, increasing influence among its Arab neighbors, and securing the return of the [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights"), have been the primary goals of Syria's foreign policy. At many points in its history, Syria has seen virulent tension with its geographically cultural neighbors, such as Turkey, Israel, Iraq, and Lebanon. Syria enjoyed an improvement in relations with several of the states in its region in the 21st century, prior to the Arab Spring and the Syrian civil war.

Since the civil war of 2011 and associated killings and human rights abuses, Syria has been increasingly isolated from the countries in the region and the wider international community. Diplomatic relations have been severed with several countries including: Britain, Canada, France, Italy, Germany, Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, United States, Belgium, Spain, and the Arab states of the Persian Gulf.[[234]](#cite_note-245)

From the Arab league, Syria continues to maintain diplomatic relations with Algeria, Egypt, Iraq, Lebanon, Sudan and Yemen. Following its violent suppression of the Arab Spring protests of the 2011 Syrian revolution, the Syrian government was suspended from the Arab League in November 2011 for over 11 years, until its reinstatement in 2023.[[123]](#cite_note-nytimes-suspend-131) Syria also quit the [Union for the Mediterranean](/wiki/Union_for_the_Mediterranean "Union for the Mediterranean").[[235]](#cite_note-246) After 11 years, the Arab League readmitted Syria.[[236]](#cite_note-247) The [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") suspended Syria in August 2012 citing "deep concern at the [massacres](/wiki/List_of_massacres_in_Syria "List of massacres in Syria") and inhuman acts" perpetrated by forces loyal to Bashar al-Assad.[[141]](#cite_note-CNN_OIC-151)

#### International disputes

See also: [Turkish occupation of northern Syria](/wiki/Turkish_occupation_of_northern_Syria "Turkish occupation of northern Syria") and [Israeli occupation of the Golan Heights](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_Golan_Heights "Israeli occupation of the Golan Heights")

In 1939, while Syria was still a French mandate the French allowed a plebiscite regarding the [Sanjak of Alexandretta](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Alexandretta "Sanjak of Alexandretta") joining to Turkey as part of a treaty of friendship in World War II. In order to facilitate this, a faulty election was done in which ethnic Turks who were originally from the Sanjak but lived in [Adana](/wiki/Adana "Adana") and other areas near the border in Turkey came to vote in the elections, shifting the election in favor of secession. Through this, the [Hatay Province](/wiki/Hatay_Province "Hatay Province") of Turkey was formed. The move by the French was very controversial in Syria, and only five years later Syria became independent.[[237]](#cite_note-Morris9-248) Despite the Turkish annexation of the Sanjak of Alexandretta, the Syrian government has refused to recognize Turkish sovereignty over the region since Independence, except for a short period in 1949.[[238]](#cite_note-AlexandrettaTurkeyRelations-249)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Golan_heights_border.jpg/250px-Golan_heights_border.jpg)](/wiki/File:Golan_heights_border.jpg)

[Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights") was occupied by Israel since the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War").

The western two-thirds of Syria's Golan Heights region are since 1967 [occupied by Israel](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") and were in 1981 [effectively annexed](/wiki/Golan_Heights_Law "Golan Heights Law") by [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"),[[239]](#cite_note-occupiedSyrian-250)[[240]](#cite_note-InternationalCommunityOccupiedTerritory-251) whereas the eastern third is controlled by Syria, with the [UNDOF](/wiki/United_Nations_Disengagement_Observer_Force "United Nations Disengagement Observer Force") maintaining a buffer zone in between, to implement the ceasefire of the [Purple Line](/wiki/Purple_Line_(ceasefire_line) "Purple Line (ceasefire line)"). Israel's 1981 Golan annexation law is not recognized in international law. The UN Security Council condemned it in Resolution 497 (1981) as "null and void and without international legal effect." Since then, General Assembly resolutions on "The Occupied Syrian Golan" reaffirm the illegality of Israeli occupation and annexation.[[241]](#cite_note-252) The Syrian government continues to demand the return of this territory.[[242]](#cite_note-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Syria.png/250px-2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Syria.png)](/wiki/File:2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Syria.png)

Israeli-occupied [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights") and [Israeli invasion of Syria](/wiki/2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Syria "2024 Israeli invasion of Syria") in December 2024

During [its invasion of Syria](/wiki/2024_Israeli_invasion_of_Syria "2024 Israeli invasion of Syria") in December 2024, Israel took control of the U.N.-patrolled buffer zone on Syrian territory, a move that violated the [1974 disengagement agreement](/wiki/Agreement_on_Disengagement_between_Israel_and_Syria "Agreement on Disengagement between Israel and Syria") with Syria.[[243]](#cite_note-254) On 23 February 2025, Israeli Prime Minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") demanded the complete demilitarization of southern Syria in the provinces of [Quneitra](/wiki/Quneitra_Governorate "Quneitra Governorate"), [Daraa](/wiki/Daraa_Governorate "Daraa Governorate") and [Sweida](/wiki/Sweida_Governorate "Sweida Governorate"), and the withdrawal of Syrian forces from Syrian territory south of Damascus.[[244]](#cite_note-255) Israeli Defense Minister [Israel Katz](/wiki/Israel_Katz "Israel Katz") said that Israeli forces would remain in southern Syria "for an indefinite period of time to protect our communities and thwart any threat."[[245]](#cite_note-256) Syria's new government under transitional President [Ahmed al-Sharaa](/wiki/Ahmed_al-Sharaa "Ahmed al-Sharaa") condemned Israel's occupation of Syrian lands and demanded Israel's withdrawal.[[246]](#cite_note-257)

The [Turkish Armed Forces](/wiki/Turkish_Armed_Forces "Turkish Armed Forces") and its ally the [Syrian National Army](/wiki/Syrian_National_Army "Syrian National Army") have [occupied areas of northern Syria](/wiki/Turkish_occupation_of_northern_Syria "Turkish occupation of northern Syria") since 2016, during the Syrian civil war.[[247]](#cite_note-258)

In early 1976, Syria entered Lebanon, beginning their 29-year [military presence](/wiki/Syrian_occupation_of_Lebanon "Syrian occupation of Lebanon"). Syria entered on the invitation of Suleiman Franjieh, the Maronite Christian president at the time to help aid the Lebanese Christian militias against the Palestinian militias.[[248]](#cite_note-St._Petersburg_Times-259)[[249]](#cite_note-260) Over the following 15 years of [Lebanese civil war](/wiki/Lebanese_Civil_War "Lebanese Civil War"), Syria fought for control over Lebanon. The Syrian military remained in Lebanon until 2005 in response to domestic and international pressure after the assassination of Lebanese Prime Minister [Rafik Hariri](/wiki/Rafik_Hariri "Rafik Hariri").[[250]](#cite_note-BBC-261)

Another disputed territory is the [Shebaa farms](/wiki/Shebaa_farms "Shebaa farms"), located in the intersection of the [Lebanese-Syrian border](/wiki/Lebanese-Syrian_border "Lebanese-Syrian border") and the Israeli-occupied Golan Heights. The farms, which are 11 km long and about 3 kilometers wide were occupied by Israel in 1981, along with rest of the Golan Heights.[[251]](#cite_note-262) Yet following Syrian army advances the Israeli occupation ended and Syria became the de facto ruling power over the farms. Yet after Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon in 2000, [Hezbollah](/wiki/Hezbollah "Hezbollah") claimed that the withdrawal was not complete because Shebaa was on Lebanese – not Syrian – territory.[[252]](#cite_note-263) After studying 81 maps, the United Nations concluded that there is no evidence of the abandoned farmlands being Lebanese.[[253]](#cite_note-264) Nevertheless, Lebanon has continued to claim ownership of the territory.

### Military

See also: [Syrian Armed Forces](/wiki/Syrian_Armed_Forces "Syrian Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Damascus_Checkpoint.jpeg/250px-Damascus_Checkpoint.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Damascus_Checkpoint.jpeg)

A [Syrian Army](/wiki/Syrian_Army "Syrian Army") soldier manning a checkpoint outside of [Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") shortly after the outbreak of the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war"), 2012

The President of Syria is commander in chief of the [Syrian Armed Forces](/wiki/Syrian_Armed_Forces "Syrian Armed Forces"), comprising some 100,000 troops upon mobilization.[[254]](#cite_note-265) Until 2024, the military operated as a conscripted force, with males required to serve 30 months upon reaching the age of 18,[[255]](#cite_note-266) though had slowly decreased over time, from in two and a half years to two years in 2005, to 21 months in 2008 and to year and a half in 2011.[[256]](#cite_note-267) Since [late 2024](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Assad_regime "Fall of the Assad regime"), compulsory military service has been inactive and is no longer enforced.

The breakup of the Soviet Union—long the principal source of training, material, and credit for the Syrian forces—may have slowed Syria's ability to acquire modern military equipment. It has an arsenal of surface-to-surface missiles. In the early 1990s, [Scud](/wiki/Scud "Scud")-C missiles with a 500-kilometre (310-mile) range were procured from North Korea, and Scud-D, with a range of up to 700 kilometres (430 miles), is allegedly being developed by Syria with the help of North Korea and Iran, according to Zisser.[[257]](#cite_note-268)

Syria received significant financial aid from [Arab states of the Persian Gulf](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf") as a result of its participation in the [Persian Gulf War](/wiki/Gulf_War "Gulf War"), with a sizable portion of these funds earmarked for military spending. Iran and Russia were the biggest suppliers of military aid to the Assad-led Syrian Government.

### Human rights

See also: [Human rights in Ba'athist Syria](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Ba%27athist_Syria "Human rights in Ba'athist Syria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/Wounded_civilians_arrive_at_hospital_Aleppo.jpg/250px-Wounded_civilians_arrive_at_hospital_Aleppo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wounded_civilians_arrive_at_hospital_Aleppo.jpg)

Wounded civilians arrive at a hospital in [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo"), October 2012

Prior to the fall of the Assad regime, the situation for [human rights in Syria](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Syria "Human rights in Syria") was a significant concern among independent organizations such as [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch"), who in 2010 referred to the country's record as "among the worst in the world."[[258]](#cite_note-269) The 2011 [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") report[[259]](#cite_note-debate-270) ranked Syria "Not Free" in its annual [Freedom in the World](/wiki/Freedom_in_the_World "Freedom in the World") survey.[[260]](#cite_note-271) The Ba'ath regime was a totalitarian dictatorship that has been internationally condemned for its domestic and political repression, including [summary executions](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Syria "Capital punishment in Syria"), [massive censorship](/wiki/Internet_censorship_in_Syria "Internet censorship in Syria"),[[261]](#cite_note-hrw-2019-272)[[262]](#cite_note-ohchr-2020-273) [forced disappearances](/wiki/Enforced_disappearance "Enforced disappearance"),[[263]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-274)[[264]](#cite_note-ohchr.org-275) etc. as well as numerous crimes against Syrian civilians perpetrated during the civil war, such as massacres, [barrel-bombings](/wiki/List_of_Syrian_civil_war_barrel_bomb_attacks "List of Syrian civil war barrel bomb attacks"), chemical attacks, etc.[[265]](#cite_note-un-chem-276)[[266]](#cite_note-press.un.org-277)

The authorities are accused of arresting democracy and human rights activists, censoring websites, detaining bloggers, and imposing travel bans. Arbitrary detention, torture, and disappearances are widespread.[[263]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-274)[[264]](#cite_note-ohchr.org-275)[[267]](#cite_note-hrw.org-278) Although Syria's constitution guarantees gender equality, critics say that personal statutes laws and the penal code discriminate against women and girls. Moreover, it also grants leniency for so-called honour killing.[[267]](#cite_note-hrw.org-278) As of 9 November 2011 during the uprising against President Bashar al-Assad, the United Nations reported that of the over 3,500 deaths, over 250 deaths were children as young as two years old, and that boys as young as 11 years old have been gang-raped by security services officers.[[268]](#cite_note-279)[[269]](#cite_note-280) [People opposing President Assad's rule](/wiki/Free_Syrian_Army "Free Syrian Army") claim that more than 200, mostly civilians, were massacred and about 300 injured in Hama in shelling by the government forces on 12 July 2012.[[270]](#cite_note-281)

In August 2013, the government was suspected of using chemical weapons against its civilians. U.S. Secretary of State [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") said it was "undeniable" that chemical weapons had been used in the country and that al-Assad's forces had committed a "moral obscenity" against his own people. "Make no mistake," Kerry said. "President Obama believes there must be accountability for those who would use the world's most heinous weapon against the world's most vulnerable people. Nothing today is more serious, and nothing is receiving more serious scrutiny".[[271]](#cite_note-282) The Emergency Law, effectively suspending most constitutional protections, was in effect from 1963 until 21 April 2011. It was justified by the government in the light of the continuing war with Israel over the Golan Heights.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86)[[217]](#cite_note-decree53-228)

In August 2014, [UN Human Rights](/wiki/Office_of_the_United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Human_Rights "Office of the United Nations High Commissioner for Human Rights") chief [Navi Pillay](/wiki/Navi_Pillay "Navi Pillay") criticized the international community over its "paralysis" in dealing with the civil war gripping the country, which by 2014 had resulted in 191,369 deaths with war crimes, according to Pillay, being committed with total impunity on all sides in the conflict. Minority Alawites and Christians were targeted by Islamists and other groups.[[272]](#cite_note-283)[[273]](#cite_note-284)[[265]](#cite_note-un-chem-276)[[266]](#cite_note-press.un.org-277) Three years later in April 2017, the U.S. Navy carried out a [missile attack](/wiki/2017_Shayrat_missile_strike "2017 Shayrat missile strike") against a Syrian air base[[274]](#cite_note-285) which had allegedly been used to conduct a [chemical weapons attack](/wiki/Khan_Shaykhun_chemical_attack "Khan Shaykhun chemical attack") on Syrian civilians, according to the U.S. government.[[275]](#cite_note-286) In November 2021, the U.S. Central Command called a 2019 airstrike that killed civilians in Syria "legitimate". The acknowledgement came after a New York Times investigation said the military had concealed the death of dozens of non-combatants.[[276]](#cite_note-287)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Syria](/wiki/Economy_of_Syria "Economy of Syria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/GDP_per_capita_development_of_Syria.svg/250px-GDP_per_capita_development_of_Syria.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_of_Syria.svg)

Historical development of real GDP per capita in Syria since 1820

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Damascus%2C_Syria%2C_The_city_square.jpg/250px-Damascus%2C_Syria%2C_The_city_square.jpg)](/wiki/File:Damascus,_Syria,_The_city_square.jpg)

[Damascus](/wiki/Damascus "Damascus") before the war, 2008

The [2011 civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war") left a negative impact on Syria's economy. However, Syria's economy began declining even before the beginning of the war. Syria's share in global exports has eroded gradually since 2001.[[277]](#cite_note-wbgrowth10-288) The real per capita GDP growth was just 2.5% per year in the 2000–2008 period.[[277]](#cite_note-wbgrowth10-288) Poverty rates increased from 11% in 2004 to 12.3% in 2007.[[277]](#cite_note-wbgrowth10-288) In 2007, main exports included crude oil, refined products, raw cotton, clothing, fruits, and grains. The bulk of imports are raw materials essential for industry, vehicles, agricultural equipment, and heavy machinery. Earnings from oil exports as well as remittances from Syrian workers are the government's most important sources of foreign exchange.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Al-Hamidiyah_Souq_02.jpg/250px-Al-Hamidiyah_Souq_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Al-Hamidiyah_Souq_02.jpg)

[Al-Hamidiyah Souq](/wiki/Al-Hamidiyah_Souq "Al-Hamidiyah Souq") in Damascus in 2010

Despite this, prior to the war, Syria was one of the most dynamic markets in the Middle East.[[278]](#cite_note-:7-289) The government visioned a transition of Syria into a free market economy.[[278]](#cite_note-:7-289) The rising oil reserves triggered rapid growth in the economy, creating business opportunities for foreign firms and investments, as the booming oil and gas industry and infrastructure projects made Syria an attractive market.[[278]](#cite_note-:7-289) Prior to the war the government hoped to attract new investment in the tourism, natural gas, and service sectors to diversify its economy and reduce its dependence on oil and agriculture.[[278]](#cite_note-:7-289) The government began to institute economic reforms aimed at liberalizing most markets, but those reforms were slow and ad hoc, and were completely reversed since the outbreak of conflict.[[279]](#cite_note-290) The [UNDP](/wiki/UNDP "UNDP") announced in 2005 that 30% of the population lived in poverty, and 11.4% live below the subsistence level.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86) At the outset of the civil war, Syria was classified by the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") as a "lower middle income country,"[[280]](#cite_note-data.worldbank.org-291) though in 2010 Syria remained dependent on the oil and [agriculture sectors](/wiki/Agriculture_sector "Agriculture sector").[[281]](#cite_note-wbbrief-292) The oil sector provided about 40% of export earnings.[[281]](#cite_note-wbbrief-292)

During the civil war, the [Syrian economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Syria "Economy of Syria") relied upon dwindling customs and income taxes which are heavily bolstered by lines of credit from Iran,[[282]](#cite_note-revenues_shrink-293) Russia and China.[[283]](#cite_note-economycrumble-294) Iran is believed to have spent between $6 billion and US$20 billion per year on Syria during the first four years of the civil war.[[284]](#cite_note-295) The [Syrian pound](/wiki/Syrian_pound "Syrian pound") lost 80% of its value, with the economy becoming part [state-owned](/wiki/State-owned_enterprise "State-owned enterprise") and part [war economy](/wiki/War_economy "War economy").[[285]](#cite_note-296) Proven [offshore](/wiki/Offshore_drilling "Offshore drilling") expeditions have indicated that large sums of oil exist on the Mediterranean Sea floor between Syria and Cyprus.[[286]](#cite_note-297) The agriculture sector contributes to about 20% of GDP and 20% of employment. Oil reserves are expected to decrease in the coming years, and Syria has become a net oil importer.[[281]](#cite_note-wbbrief-292) As of 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Syria&action=edit), the value of overall exports was slashed by two-thirds, from the figure of US$12 billion in 2010 to only US$4 billion in 2012.[[287]](#cite_note-AFP_economy-298)

Since 2012, oil and tourism industries in particular have been devastated, with $5 billion lost.[[287]](#cite_note-AFP_economy-298) Sanctions have sapped the government's finances. U.S. and European Union bans on oil imports, which went into effect in 2012, are estimated to cost Syria about $400 million per month.[[288]](#cite_note-shortap-299) Around 40% of all employees in the tourism sector lost their jobs since the beginning of the war.[[289]](#cite_note-economyhang-300) In May 2015, [ISIS](/wiki/ISIS "ISIS") captured Syria's phosphate mines, one of the Syrian government's last chief sources of income.[[290]](#cite_note-301) The following month, ISIS blew up a gas pipeline to Damascus that was used to generate heating and electricity in Damascus and Homs; "the name of its game for now is denial of key resources to the regime" an analyst stated.[[291]](#cite_note-302) In addition, ISIS was closing in on [Shaer gas field](/wiki/Shaer_gas_field "Shaer gas field") and three other facilities in the area—Hayan, Jihar and Ebla—with the loss of these western gas fields having the potential to cause Iran to further subsidize the Syrian government.[[292]](#cite_note-303) Reconstruction will cost as much as $10 billion.[[287]](#cite_note-AFP_economy-298) [Aleppo soap](/wiki/Aleppo_soap "Aleppo soap") is a popular product of Syria.

The economy was highly regulated by the government, which increased subsidies and tightened trade controls to assuage protesters and protect [foreign currency reserves](/wiki/Foreign_currency_reserve "Foreign currency reserve").[[2]](#cite_note-CIA-5) Long-run economic constraints include foreign trade barriers, declining oil production, high unemployment, rising budget deficits, and increasing pressure on water supplies caused by heavy use in agriculture, rapid population growth, industrial expansion, and water pollution.[[2]](#cite_note-CIA-5) In 2024, the World Bank estimated that the Syrian GDP had contracted by 84% from 2010 to 2023. As of 2023, its GDP was $6.2 billion. Syria's new government has vowed to stamp out the production of captagon, which makes up a major portion of the informal economy. The new government will also face challenges in securing and resuming the production of oil, as rebuilding the industry will require significant investments with no guarantee of a return.[[293]](#cite_note-304)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Beach_Latakia_4.jpg/250px-Beach_Latakia_4.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beach_Latakia_4.jpg)

A cove in [Latakia](/wiki/Latakia "Latakia") in 2014

### Energy

This section is an excerpt from [Energy in Syria](/wiki/Energy_in_Syria "Energy in Syria").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Energy_in_Syria&action=edit)]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/%E6%95%98%E5%88%A9%E4%BA%9E%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F137.jpg/250px-%E6%95%98%E5%88%A9%E4%BA%9E%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F137.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%95%98%E5%88%A9%E4%BA%9E%E5%8F%A4%E8%B9%9F137.jpg)

Pumpjack

[Energy](/wiki/Primary_energy "Primary energy") in Syria is mostly based on [oil](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum") and [gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas").[[294]](#cite_note-Energy_in_Syria_:1-305) Some energy infrastructure was damaged by the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war"). There is high reliance on [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuel "Fossil fuel") for energy in Syria,[[295]](#cite_note-306) and [electricity](/wiki/Electricity_in_Syria "Electricity in Syria") demand is projected to increase by 2030, especially for industry activity such as [automation](/wiki/Automation "Automation").[[296]](#cite_note-307) However, conflict in Syria has caused electricity generation to decrease by nearly 40% in recent years due to plant destruction and fuel shortages.[[297]](#cite_note-308) Electricity access in daily life for Syrians has also been altered due to conflict. Electricity to residents of Syria is largely provided by private [diesel generators](/wiki/Diesel_generator "Diesel generator"), which is costly and limited in hours of use.[[298]](#cite_note-Energy_in_Syria_:13-309) Conflict has increased household electricity expenditures while also decreasing [household income](/wiki/Disposable_household_and_per_capita_income "Disposable household and per capita income").[[298]](#cite_note-Energy_in_Syria_:13-309) Some households have since turned to [solar energy](/wiki/Solar_Energy_Generating_Systems "Solar Energy Generating Systems") as a supplementary source of energy, though high costs limit widespread adoption.[[298]](#cite_note-Energy_in_Syria_:13-309)

### Agriculture

This section is an excerpt from [Agriculture in Syria](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Syria "Agriculture in Syria").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Agriculture_in_Syria&action=edit)]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(February 2026)* |

Agriculture is a key part of the [economy of Syria](/wiki/Economy_of_Syria "Economy of Syria"). The sector still accounts for an estimated 26 percent of gross domestic product (GDP) and represents a critical safety net for the 6.7 million Syrians – including those internally displaced – who still remain in rural areas. However, agriculture and the livelihoods that depend on it have suffered massive losses. Today, food production is at a record low and around half the population remaining in Syria are unable to meet their daily food needs.[[299]](#cite_note-310)

Until the mid-1970s, [agriculture in Syria](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Syria "Agriculture in Syria") was the primary [economic activity in Syria](/wiki/Economy_of_Syria "Economy of Syria"). At independence in 1946, [agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") (including minor [forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry") and [fishing](/wiki/Fishing "Fishing")) was the most important sector of the economy, and in the 1940s and early 1950s, agriculture was the fastest growing sector. Wealthy merchants from urban centers such as Aleppo invested in [land development](/wiki/Land_development "Land development") and irrigation. The rapid expansion of the cultivated area and increased output stimulated the rest of the economy. However, by the late 1950s, there was little land left that could easily be brought under cultivation. During the 1960s, [agricultural output](/wiki/Agricultural_productivity "Agricultural productivity") stagnated because of political instability and [land reform](/wiki/Land_reform "Land reform"). Between 1953 and 1976, agriculture's contribution to [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP") increased (in constant prices) by only 3.2%, approximately the rate of population growth. From 1976 to 1984 growth in agriculture declined to 2% a year, and its importance in the economy declined as other sectors grew more rapidly.

In 1981, as in the 1970s, 53% of the population was still classified as [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural"), although movement to the cities continued to accelerate. However, in contrast to the 1970s, when 50% of the [labor force](/wiki/Labor_force "Labor force") was employed in agriculture, by 1983 agriculture employed only 30% of the labor force. Furthermore, by the mid-1980s, unprocessed farm products accounted for only 4% of exports, equivalent to 7% of non-petroleum exports. Industry, commerce, and transportation still depended on [farm produce](/wiki/Produce "Produce") and related [agro-business](/wiki/Agro-business "Agro-business"), but agriculture's preeminent position had clearly eroded. By 1985 agriculture (including a little forestry and fishing) contributed only 16.5% to GDP, down from 22.1% in 1976.

By the mid-1980s, the [Syrian government](/wiki/Syrian_government "Syrian government") had taken measures to revitalize agriculture. The 1985 investment budget saw a sharp rise in allocations for agriculture, including [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") and [irrigation](/wiki/Irrigation "Irrigation"). The government's renewed commitment to agricultural development in the 1980s, by expanding cultivation and extending irrigation, promised brighter prospects for Syrian agriculture in the 1990s.

During the [Syrian Civil War](/wiki/Syrian_Civil_War "Syrian Civil War"), the agricultural sector has witnessed a drop in producing all kinds of commodities such as wheat, cotton and olives,[[300]](#cite_note-311) due to the lack of security and immigration of agricultural workforce,[[301]](#cite_note-312) especially in [Al-Hasakah Governorate](/wiki/Al-Hasakah_Governorate "Al-Hasakah Governorate") and [Aleppo Governorate](/wiki/Aleppo_Governorate "Aleppo Governorate").

### Transport

Main articles: [Transport in Syria](/wiki/Transport_in_Syria "Transport in Syria") and [Rail transport in Syria](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Syria "Rail transport in Syria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/%D8%AC%D8%B3%D8%B1_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B1%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%86_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%83%D8%A8%D9%8A%D8%B1.jpg/250px-%D8%AC%D8%B3%D8%B1_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B1%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%86_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%83%D8%A8%D9%8A%D8%B1.jpg)](/wiki/File:%D8%AC%D8%B3%D8%B1_%D8%A7%D9%84%D8%B1%D8%B3%D8%AA%D9%86_%D8%A7%D9%84%D9%83%D8%A8%D9%8A%D8%B1.jpg)

Expressway M5 near [Al-Rastan](/wiki/Al-Rastan "Al-Rastan"), [Homs](/wiki/Homs_Governorate "Homs Governorate")

Syria has four international airports ([Damascus](/wiki/Damascus_International_Airport "Damascus International Airport"), [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo_International_Airport "Aleppo International Airport"), [Latakia](/wiki/Latakia_International_Airport "Latakia International Airport") and [Qamishli](/wiki/Qamishli_International_Airport "Qamishli International Airport")), which serve as hubs for [Syrian Air](/wiki/Syrian_Air "Syrian Air") and [Fly Cham](/wiki/Fly_Cham "Fly Cham") and are also served by a variety of foreign carriers.[[302]](#cite_note-313) Turkey restored commercial flights to Syria after the fall of the Assad regime.[[303]](#cite_note-DailySabah-05212025-314)

Syria has 2,850 kilometres (1,770 mi) of [railways](/wiki/Syrian_Railways "Syrian Railways"), out of which 1,500 kilometres (930 mi) are out of operation in 2025 because of damage sustained during the civil war. The [rolling stock](/wiki/Rolling_stock "Rolling stock") is also in a state of disrepair.[[303]](#cite_note-DailySabah-05212025-314) As of 2024 there are no international rail services, but [high-speed rail in Turkey](/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Turkey "High-speed rail in Turkey") is being extended close to the border.[[304]](#cite_note-315) The last train between Syria and Jordan operated in September 2011, and the transitional government plans to rebuild the Syrian section of the [Hejaz Jordan Railway](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Jordan#Hedjaz_Jordan_Railway "Rail transport in Jordan").[[305]](#cite_note-316) The first trial run of a passenger train between Aleppo and Hama took place in August 2025 after months of renovation work, with plans to restore regular service between the two cities.[[306]](#cite_note-317)

The road network in Syria is 69,873 kilometres (43,417 miles) long, including 1,103 kilometres (685 miles) of expressways. The country also has 900 kilometres (560 miles) of navigable but not economically significant waterways.[[2]](#cite_note-CIA-5)

### Internet and telecommunications

[Telecommunications in Syria](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Syria "Telecommunications in Syria") are overseen by the [Ministry of Communications and Information Technology](/wiki/Ministry_of_Communications_and_Information_Technology_(Syria) "Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (Syria)").[[307]](#cite_note-318) In addition, [Syrian Telecom](/wiki/Syrian_Telecom "Syrian Telecom") plays an integral role in the distribution of internet access.[[308]](#cite_note-319)

During the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war") the [Syrian Electronic Army](/wiki/Syrian_Electronic_Army "Syrian Electronic Army") served as a pro-[Assad regime](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Syria "Ba'athist Syria") military faction in cyberspace and was long considered an enemy of the [hacktivist](/wiki/Hacktivism "Hacktivism") group [Anonymous](/wiki/Anonymous_(hacker_group) "Anonymous (hacker group)").[[309]](#cite_note-320) Because of [internet censorship](/wiki/Internet_censorship "Internet censorship") laws, 13,000 internet activists were arrested in 2011 and 2012.[[310]](#cite_note-321)

### Water supply and sanitation

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in Syria](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Syria "Water supply and sanitation in Syria")

Syria is a semiarid country with scarce water resources. The largest water consuming sector in Syria is agriculture. Domestic water use stands at only about 9% of total water use.[[311]](#cite_note-Agriculture-322) A big challenge for Syria before the civil war was its high population growth (in 2006 the growth rate was 2.7%[[312]](#cite_note-323)), leading to rapidly increasing demand for urban and industrial water.[[313]](#cite_note-World_Bank-324)

### Drug industry

Main article: [Syrian Captagon industry](/wiki/Syrian_Captagon_industry "Syrian Captagon industry")

Prior to the fall of the Ba'athist regime on 8 December 2024, Syria was home to a burgeoning [illegal drugs](/wiki/Prohibition_of_drugs "Prohibition of drugs") industry run by associates and relatives of Bashar al-Assad.[[314]](#cite_note-:0-325) It mainly produced [captagon](/wiki/Captagon "Captagon"), an addictive [amphetamine](/wiki/Amphetamine "Amphetamine") popular in the Arab world. As of 2021, the export of illegal drugs eclipsed the country's legal exports, leading the *New York Times* to call Syria "the world's newest [narcostate](/wiki/Narco-state "Narco-state")".[[314]](#cite_note-:0-325) The drug exports allow the government to generate hard currency and bypass [international sanctions](/wiki/International_sanctions_against_Syria "International sanctions against Syria").[[314]](#cite_note-:0-325)[[315]](#cite_note-326)[[316]](#cite_note-auto-327) Captagon was Syria's primary export, valued at a minimum of US$3.4 billion annually, surpassing the country's largest legal export, olive oil, which is valued at around US$122 million per year.[[317]](#cite_note-328)

Most people live in the [Euphrates River valley](/wiki/Euphrates "Euphrates") and along the coastal plain, a fertile strip between the coastal mountains and the desert. Overall population density before the civil war was about 99 per square kilometre (258 per square mile).[[320]](#cite_note-331) According to the *World Refugee Survey 2008*, published by the U.S. Committee for Refugees and Immigrants, Syria hosted a population of refugees and asylum seekers numbering approximately 1,852,300. The vast majority of this population was from Iraq (1,300,000), but sizeable populations from [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)") (543,400) and [Somalia](/wiki/Somalia "Somalia") (5,200) also lived in the country.[[321]](#cite_note-World_Refugee_Survey_2008-332)

In what the UN has described as "the biggest humanitarian emergency of our era",[[322]](#cite_note-333) by 2014 about 9.5 million Syrians, half the population, had been displaced since March 2011;[[323]](#cite_note-Reuters_Half-334) 4 million were outside the country as refugees.[[136]](#cite_note-UNHCR-145) By 2020, the UN estimated that over 5.5 million Syrians were living as refugees in the region, and 6.1 million others were internally displaced.[[324]](#cite_note-The_Refugee_Brief_2020-335)
