### Ethnic groups

Main article: [Syrians](/wiki/Syrians "Syrians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Damascus%2C_traditional_clothing_%286364877017%29.jpg/250px-Damascus%2C_traditional_clothing_%286364877017%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Damascus,_traditional_clothing_(6364877017).jpg)

Damascus, traditional clothing

Syrians are an overall indigenous [Levantine](/wiki/Levant "Levant") people, closely related to their immediate neighbors, such as [Lebanese](/wiki/Lebanese_people "Lebanese people"), [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians"), [Jordanians](/wiki/Demographics_of_Jordan "Demographics of Jordan") and [Jews](/wiki/Jews "Jews").[[325]](#cite_note-336)[[326]](#cite_note-337) Syria has a population of approximately 26,000,000 (2025 estimate). [Syrian Arabs](/wiki/Syrian_people "Syrian people"), together with some 600,000 [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinians_in_Syria "Palestinians in Syria") not including the 6 million refugees outside the country make up roughly 74% of the population.[[2]](#cite_note-CIA-5) The indigenous [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrians_in_Syria "Assyrians in Syria") and [Western Aramaic](/wiki/Western_Neo-Aramaic "Western Neo-Aramaic")-speakers number around 400,000 people,[[327]](#cite_note-338) with the Western Aramaic-speakers living mainly in the villages of [Maaloula](/wiki/Maaloula "Maaloula"), [Jubb'adin](/wiki/Jubb%27adin "Jubb'adin") and [Bakh'a](/wiki/Al-Sarkha_(Bakhah) "Al-Sarkha (Bakhah)"), while the Assyrians mainly reside in the north and northeast (Homs, Aleppo, Qamishli, Hasakah). Many (particularly the Assyrian group) still retain several [Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/Neo-Aramaic "Neo-Aramaic") dialects as spoken and written languages.[[328]](#cite_note-339)

The second-largest ethnic group in Syria are the [Kurds](/wiki/Kurds_in_Syria "Kurds in Syria"). They constitute about 9%[[329]](#cite_note-SyriaKurds-340) to 10%[[8]](#cite_note-Khalifa2013-12) of the population, or approximately 2 million people (including 40,000 [Yazidis](/wiki/Yazidis_in_Syria "Yazidis in Syria")[[8]](#cite_note-Khalifa2013-12)). Most Kurds reside in the northeastern corner of Syria and most speak the [Kurmanji](/wiki/Kurmanji "Kurmanji") variant of the [Kurdish language](/wiki/Kurdish_language "Kurdish language").[[329]](#cite_note-SyriaKurds-340) The third largest ethnic group are the [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language")-speaking [Syrian Turkmen](/wiki/Syrian_Turkmen "Syrian Turkmen")/Turkoman. There are no reliable estimates of their total population, with estimates ranging from several hundred thousand to 3.5 million.[[330]](#cite_note-341)[[331]](#cite_note-342)[[332]](#cite_note-Turkmenistan-343) The fourth largest ethnic group are the [Assyrians](/wiki/Syrian-Assyrians "Syrian-Assyrians") (3–4%),[[8]](#cite_note-Khalifa2013-12) followed by the [Circassians](/wiki/Circassians_in_Syria "Circassians in Syria") (1.5%)[[8]](#cite_note-Khalifa2013-12) and the [Armenians](/wiki/Armenians_in_Syria "Armenians in Syria") (1%),[[8]](#cite_note-Khalifa2013-12) most of which are the descendants of refugees who arrived in Syria during the [Armenian genocide](/wiki/Armenian_genocide "Armenian genocide"). Syria holds the [7th largest Armenian population in the world](/wiki/Armenian_diaspora "Armenian diaspora"). They are mainly gathered in Aleppo, [Qamishli](/wiki/Qamishli "Qamishli"), Damascus and [Kesab](/wiki/Kesab "Kesab").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Syria_Ethno-religious_Map.svg/250px-Syria_Ethno-religious_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Syria_Ethno-religious_Map.svg)

The ethno-religious composition of Syria

There are also smaller ethnic minority groups, such as the [Albanians](/wiki/Albanians "Albanians"), [Bosnians](/wiki/Bosnians "Bosnians"), [Georgians](/wiki/Georgians "Georgians"), [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks_in_Syria "Greeks in Syria"), [Persians](/wiki/Persians "Persians"), [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns") and [Russians](/wiki/Russians "Russians").[[8]](#cite_note-Khalifa2013-12) However, most of these ethnic minorities have become [Arabized](/wiki/Arabized "Arabized") to some degree, particularly those who practice the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") faith.[[8]](#cite_note-Khalifa2013-12) The largest concentration of the [Syrian diaspora](/wiki/Syrian_diaspora "Syrian diaspora") outside the [Arab world](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world") is in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), which has millions of people of Arab and other Near Eastern ancestries.[[333]](#cite_note-344) Brazil is the first country in the Americas to offer humanitarian visas to Syrian refugees.[[334]](#cite_note-345) The majority of [Arab Argentines](/wiki/Arab_Argentine "Arab Argentine") are from either Lebanese or Syrian background.[[335]](#cite_note-346)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Syria](/wiki/Languages_of_Syria "Languages of Syria")

[Arabic](/wiki/Modern_Standard_Arabic "Modern Standard Arabic") is the [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") of the country.[[336]](#cite_note-CoSAR-347) Several modern [Arabic dialects](/wiki/Varieties_of_Arabic "Varieties of Arabic") are used in everyday life, most notably [Levantine](/wiki/Levantine_Arabic "Levantine Arabic") in the west and [Mesopotamian](/wiki/Mesopotamian_Arabic "Mesopotamian Arabic") in the northeast. According to *The Encyclopedia of Arabic Language and Linguistics*, in addition to Arabic, the following languages are spoken in the country, in order of the number of speakers: [Kurdish](/wiki/Kurdish_language "Kurdish language") (recognized in 2026 as a [national language](/wiki/National_language "National language") of Syria),[[4]](#cite_note-al-monitor2026-01-16-7)[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348) [Turkish](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"),[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348) [Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/Neo-Aramaic_languages "Neo-Aramaic languages") (four dialects),[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348), [Circassian](/wiki/Circassian_languages "Circassian languages"),[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348) [Chechen](/wiki/Chechen_language "Chechen language"),[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348) [Armenian](/wiki/Armenian_language "Armenian language"),[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348) and finally [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language").[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348) Besides Kurdish, none of these minority languages have official status.[[337]](#cite_note-Behnstedt-348)

Many educated Syrians speak [English](/wiki/English_language "English language"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"), and [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") (especially among the bourgeoisie and the [Christian community](/wiki/Christianity_in_Syria "Christianity in Syria"); there are fewer than 4,500 [French speakers](/wiki/Geographical_distribution_of_French_speakers "Geographical distribution of French speakers") today), but English is more widely understood (between 650,000 and one million speakers, as a second language). Before the start of the [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war"), around 8,000 students were enrolled in French-speaking Catholic schools.[[338]](#cite_note-349)

[Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") was the [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") of the region before the advent of [Arabic](/wiki/Classical_Arabic "Classical Arabic"), and is still spoken among [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people"), and [Classical Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language") is still used as the liturgical language of [various Syriac Christian denominations](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity"). Most remarkably, [Western Neo-Aramaic](/wiki/Western_Neo-Aramaic "Western Neo-Aramaic") is still spoken in the village of [Maaloula](/wiki/Maaloula "Maaloula") as well as two neighboring villages, 56 km (35 mi) northeast of Damascus. English and French are widely spoken as second languages, but English is more often used.[[339]](#cite_note-350)

### Religion

Main articles: [Religion in Syria](/wiki/Religion_in_Syria "Religion in Syria"), [Islam in Syria](/wiki/Islam_in_Syria "Islam in Syria"), and [Christianity in Syria](/wiki/Christianity_in_Syria "Christianity in Syria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/The_Umayyad_square.jpg/250px-The_Umayyad_square.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Umayyad_square.jpg)

[Umayyad Mosque](/wiki/Umayyad_Mosque "Umayyad Mosque")

[Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Syria "Islam in Syria") is the largest and predominant religion in Syria, comprising 87% of the population. [Sunni Muslims](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") make up around 74% of the population[[2]](#cite_note-CIA-5) and Sunni Arabs account for 59–60%. Most Kurds (8.5%)[[340]](#cite_note-Drysdale&Hinnebusch1991-351) and most Turkmens (3%)[[340]](#cite_note-Drysdale&Hinnebusch1991-351) are Sunni, while 3% of Syrians are [Shia Muslims](/wiki/Shia_Islam "Shia Islam") (particularly [Ismailis](/wiki/Ismailism "Ismailism"), and [Twelvers](/wiki/Twelver_Shi%27ism "Twelver Shi'ism") but there are also Arabs, Kurds and Turkmens), 10% are [Alawites](/wiki/Alawites "Alawites"), 10% are [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians")[[2]](#cite_note-CIA-5) (the majority are Antiochian Greek Orthodox, the rest are Syriac Orthodox, Greek Catholic and other Catholic Rites, Armenian Orthodox, Assyrian Church of the East, Protestants and other denominations), and 3% [Druzes](/wiki/Druze "Druze").[[2]](#cite_note-CIA-5) Druze number around 500,000 and concentrate mainly in the southern area of [Jabal al-Druze](/wiki/Jabal_al-Druze "Jabal al-Druze").[[341]](#cite_note-book-352) According to the [Association of Religion Data Archives](/wiki/Association_of_Religion_Data_Archives "Association of Religion Data Archives") (ARDA), 94.17% of Syrians are Muslims–79.19% are Sunnis and 14.10% are Shias (including Alawites)–and 3.84% of Syrians are Christians as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Syria&action=edit).[[10]](#cite_note-:4-14)

Because the [Assad family](/wiki/Assad_family "Assad family") is Alawite, Alawites have historically dominated key government and military positions.[[100]](#cite_note-The_Sturdy_House_That_Assad_Built2-108)[[342]](#cite_note-353)[[343]](#cite_note-354)

Christians numbering 1.2 million, a sizable number of whom are found among Syria's population of Palestinian and Iraqi refugees, are divided into several sects. The [Greek Orthodox](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church_of_Antioch "Greek Orthodox Church of Antioch") make up 45.7% of the Christian population; the [Syriac Orthodox](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church") make up 22.4%; the [Armenian Orthodox](/wiki/Armenian_Apostolic_Church "Armenian Apostolic Church") make up 10.9%; the Catholics (including [Greek Catholic](/wiki/Melkite_Greek_Catholic_Church "Melkite Greek Catholic Church"), [Syriac Catholic](/wiki/Syriac_Catholic_Church "Syriac Catholic Church"), [Armenian Catholic](/wiki/Armenian_Catholic_Church "Armenian Catholic Church"), [Maronite](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church"), [Chaldean Catholic](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church") and [Latin](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites")) make up 16.2%; [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East") and several smaller Christian denominations account for the remainder. Many Christian [monasteries](/wiki/List_of_Monasteries_in_Syria "List of Monasteries in Syria") also exist. Many Christian Syrians belong to a high socio-economic class.[[344]](#cite_note-355) As per one estimate, the count of Christians affiliated with established denominations in Syria has dropped from approximately 2.5 million before the civil war, to about 500,000 in 2023.[[345]](#cite_note-356)

Syria was once home to a substantial population of [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Syria "History of the Jews in Syria"), with large communities in Damascus, Aleppo, and [Qamishii](/wiki/Qamishli "Qamishli").[[346]](#cite_note-:5-357) Due to a combination of persecution in Syria and opportunities elsewhere, the Jews began to emigrate in the second half of the 19th century to Great Britain, the United States, and Israel.[[346]](#cite_note-:5-357) The process was completed with the establishment of Israel in 1948.[[346]](#cite_note-:5-357) The remaining Jewish population dwindled as a result of the civil war. Today 100 Jews live in Syria.[[346]](#cite_note-:5-357) The United States is home to a large Syrian Jewish community, which is still considered as Syrian citizens by the Syrian government.

### Education

Main article: [Education in Syria](/wiki/Education_in_Syria "Education in Syria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Aleppo_University%2C_Faculty_of_Arts_and_Humanities.jpg/250px-Aleppo_University%2C_Faculty_of_Arts_and_Humanities.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aleppo_University,_Faculty_of_Arts_and_Humanities.jpg)

Faculty of Arts and Humanities in [Aleppo University](/wiki/Aleppo_University "Aleppo University")

Education is free and compulsory from ages 6 to 12. Schooling consists of six years of primary education followed by a three-year general or [vocational](/wiki/Vocation "Vocation") training period and a three-year academic or vocational program. The second three-year period of academic training is required for university [admission](/wiki/University_and_college_admission "University and college admission"). Total enrollment at [post-secondary](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education") schools is over 150,000. The [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") rate of Syrians aged 15 and older is 90.7% for males and 82.2% for females.[[79]](#cite_note-USDOS-86)[[347]](#cite_note-impact-se-358)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/UIS_Literacy_Rate_Syria_population_plus15_1980_2015.png/250px-UIS_Literacy_Rate_Syria_population_plus15_1980_2015.png)](/wiki/File:UIS_Literacy_Rate_Syria_population_plus15_1980_2015.png)

[UIS](/wiki/UNESCO_Institute_for_Statistics "UNESCO Institute for Statistics") adult literacy rate of Syria

There are ten public universities in Syria and 39 private universities.[[348]](#cite_note-359) The top two state universities are [Damascus University](/wiki/Damascus_University "Damascus University") (210,000 students as of 2014)[[349]](#cite_note-360) and [University of Aleppo](/wiki/University_of_Aleppo "University of Aleppo").[[350]](#cite_note-361) The top private universities in Syria are: [Syrian Private University](/wiki/Syrian_Private_University "Syrian Private University"), [Arab International University](/wiki/Arab_International_University "Arab International University"), [University of Kalamoon](/wiki/University_of_Kalamoon "University of Kalamoon") and [International University for Science and Technology](/wiki/International_University_for_Science_and_Technology "International University for Science and Technology"). There are also many higher institutes in Syria, like the [Higher Institute of Business Administration](/wiki/Higher_Institute_of_Business_Administration "Higher Institute of Business Administration"), which offer undergraduate and graduate programs in business.[[351]](#cite_note-362)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Syria](/wiki/Health_in_Syria "Health in Syria")

The [Syrian civil war](/wiki/Syrian_civil_war "Syrian civil war") profoundly impacted the nation's health demographics and infrastructure.[[352]](#cite_note-:8-363) As of 2023, the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO) estimated Syria's population at approximately 23.6 million.[[353]](#cite_note-:9-364)

In 2021, the average life expectancy in Syria was 73.1 years.[[353]](#cite_note-:9-364) The infant mortality rate has seen a decline over recent years, with estimates indicating 12.1 deaths per 1,000 live births in 2022.[[354]](#cite_note-365) The under-five mortality rate was reported at 21.4 deaths per 1,000 live births.[[355]](#cite_note-366)

Prior to the conflict, Syria had a maternal mortality ratio of 52 deaths per 100,000 live births in 2009.[[352]](#cite_note-:8-363) However, the war disrupted maternal healthcare services, leading to increased risks for pregnant women.

The protracted conflict severely damaged Syria's healthcare infrastructure. In 2023 over half of the country's hospitals were non-functional, and approximately 15.3 million people were in need of health support, representing 72% of the population. This marked a 25% increase from 2022, highlighting the escalating health crisis.[[356]](#cite_note-367)[[357]](#cite_note-:10-368)

Non-communicable diseases, particularly [cardiovascular conditions](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"), were leading causes of death before the conflict. However, during the war, conflict-related injuries and deaths surged, significantly altering the health landscape.[[352]](#cite_note-:8-363)[[357]](#cite_note-:10-368)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Syria](/wiki/Culture_of_Syria "Culture of Syria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Dabke2.jpg/250px-Dabke2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dabke2.jpg)

[Dabke](/wiki/Dabke "Dabke") combines circle dance and line dancing and is widely performed at weddings and other joyous occasions.

Syria is a traditional society with a long cultural history.[[358]](#cite_note-369) Importance is placed on family, religion, education, self-discipline and respect. Syrians' taste for the traditional arts is expressed in dances such as the al-Samah, the [Dabkeh](/wiki/Dabkeh "Dabkeh") in all their variations, and the sword dance. Marriage ceremonies and the births of children are occasions for the lively demonstration of folk customs.[[359]](#cite_note-traditional-370)

### Literature

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Adonis_Cracow_Poland_May12_2011_Fot_Mariusz_Kubik_08.JPG/250px-Adonis_Cracow_Poland_May12_2011_Fot_Mariusz_Kubik_08.JPG)](/wiki/File:Adonis_Cracow_Poland_May12_2011_Fot_Mariusz_Kubik_08.JPG)

Poet, essayist and translator [Adunis](/wiki/Adunis "Adunis")

The [literature of Syria](/wiki/Syrian_literature "Syrian literature") has contributed to [Arabic literature](/wiki/Arabic_literature "Arabic literature") and has a proud tradition of oral and written poetry. Syrian writers, many of whom migrated to Egypt, played a crucial role in the [nahda](/wiki/Nahda "Nahda") or Arab literary and cultural revival of the 19th century. Prominent contemporary Syrian writers include, among others, [Adonis](/wiki/Ali_Ahmad_Said "Ali Ahmad Said"), [Muhammad Maghout](/wiki/Muhammad_al-Maghut "Muhammad al-Maghut"), [Haidar Haidar](/wiki/Haidar_Haidar "Haidar Haidar"), [Ghada al-Samman](/wiki/Ghada_al-Samman "Ghada al-Samman"), [Nizar Qabbani](/wiki/Nizar_Qabbani "Nizar Qabbani") and [Zakariyya Tamer](/wiki/Zakariyya_Tamer "Zakariyya Tamer").

Ba'ath Party rule brought about renewed censorship.[[261]](#cite_note-hrw-2019-272)[[262]](#cite_note-ohchr-2020-273) In this context, the genre of the historical novel, spearheaded by Nabil Sulayman, [Fawwaz Haddad](/wiki/Fawwaz_Haddad "Fawwaz Haddad"), Khyri al-Dhahabi and Nihad Siris, is sometimes used as a means of expressing dissent, critiquing the present through a depiction of the past. Syrian [folk narrative](/wiki/List_of_folklores "List of folklores"), as a subgenre of historical fiction, is imbued with [magical realism](/wiki/Magical_realism "Magical realism"), and is also used as a means of veiled criticism of the present. [Salim Barakat](/wiki/Salim_Barakat "Salim Barakat"), a Syrian émigré living in Sweden, is one of the leading figures of the genre. Contemporary Syrian literature also encompasses science fiction and futuristic [utopiae](/wiki/Utopia "Utopia") (Nuhad Sharif, Talib Umran), which may also serve as media of dissent.

### Music

The [Syrian music](/wiki/Music_of_Syria "Music of Syria") scene, in particular that of Damascus, has long been among the Arab world's most important, especially in the field of [classical Arab music](/wiki/Classical_Arab_music "Classical Arab music"). Syria has produced several pan-Arab stars, including [Asmahan](/wiki/Asmahan "Asmahan"), [Farid al-Atrash](/wiki/Farid_al-Atrash "Farid al-Atrash") and singer [Lena Chamamyan](/wiki/Lena_Chamamyan "Lena Chamamyan"). The city of Aleppo is known for its [muwashshah](/wiki/Muwashshah "Muwashshah"), a form of [Andalous](/wiki/Andalous "Andalous") sung poetry popularized by [Sabri Moudallal](/wiki/Sabri_Moudallal "Sabri Moudallal"), as well as for popular stars like [Sabah Fakhri](/wiki/Sabah_Fakhri "Sabah Fakhri").

### Media

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/ff/Suzan_Najm_Aldeen_-_Sep_10%2C_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Suzan_Najm_Aldeen_-_Sep_10,_2018.jpg)

[Suzan Najm Aldeen](/wiki/Suzan_Najm_Aldeen "Suzan Najm Aldeen"), Syrian actress

[Television was introduced to Syria](/wiki/Television_in_Syria "Television in Syria") and Egypt in 1960, when both were part of the [United Arab Republic](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic"). It broadcast in black and white until 1976.[[360]](#cite_note-:6-371) [Syrian soap operas](/wiki/Syrian_soap_operas "Syrian soap operas") have considerable market penetration throughout the eastern Arab world.[[360]](#cite_note-:6-371)

Nearly all of [Syria's media](/wiki/Media_of_Syria "Media of Syria") outlets are state-owned.[[361]](#cite_note-372) The authorities operate several intelligence agencies,[[362]](#cite_note-373) among them [Shu'bat al-Mukhabarat al-'Askariyya](/wiki/Military_Intelligence_(Syria) "Military Intelligence (Syria)"), employing many operatives.[[363]](#cite_note-374) During the civil war many of Syria's artists, poets, writers and activists have been incarcerated, and some have been killed, including famed cartoonist [Akram Raslan](/wiki/Akram_Raslam "Akram Raslam").[[364]](#cite_note-375)

### Cuisine

[Syrian cuisine](/wiki/Syrian_cuisine "Syrian cuisine") is rich and varied in its ingredients, linked to the regions where a specific dish has originated. Syrian food mostly consists of southern Mediterranean, Greek, and Southwest Asian dishes. Some Syrian dishes also evolved from Turkish and French cooking: dishes like [shish kebab](/wiki/Kebab "Kebab"), stuffed zucchini/courgette, and *[yabraʾ](/wiki/Dolma "Dolma")* (stuffed grape leaves, the word *yabraʾ* deriving from the Turkish word *yaprak*, meaning leaf).

The main dishes are [kibbeh](/wiki/Kibbeh "Kibbeh"), [hummus](/wiki/Hummus "Hummus"), [tabbouleh](/wiki/Tabbouleh "Tabbouleh"), [fattoush](/wiki/Fattoush "Fattoush"), [labneh](/wiki/Strained_yogurt "Strained yogurt"), [shawarma](/wiki/Shawarma "Shawarma"), [mujaddara](/wiki/Mujaddara "Mujaddara"), [shanklish](/wiki/Shanklish "Shanklish"), [pastırma](/wiki/Past%C4%B1rma "Pastırma"), [sujuk](/wiki/Sujuk "Sujuk") and baklava. [Baklava](/wiki/Baklava "Baklava") is made of [filo](/wiki/Filo "Filo") pastry filled with chopped nuts and soaked in [honey](/wiki/Honey "Honey"). Syrians often serve selections of appetizers, known as [meze](/wiki/Meze "Meze"), before the main course. [Za'atar](/wiki/Za%27atar "Za'atar"), [minced beef](/wiki/Minced_beef "Minced beef"), and cheese [manakish](/wiki/Manakish "Manakish") are popular [hors d'œuvres](/wiki/Hors_d%27%C5%93uvres "Hors d'œuvres"). The Arabic flatbread [khubz](/wiki/Khubz "Khubz") is always eaten together with meze.

Drinks vary, depending on the time of day and the occasion. [Arabic coffee](/wiki/Arabic_coffee "Arabic coffee") is the most well-known hot drink, usually prepared in the morning at breakfast or in the evening. It is usually served for guests or after food. [Arak](/wiki/Arak_(distilled_beverage) "Arak (distilled beverage)"), an alcoholic drink, is a well-known beverage, served mostly on special occasions. Other Syrian beverages include [ayran](/wiki/Ayran "Ayran"), [jallab](/wiki/Jallab "Jallab"), [white coffee](/wiki/White_coffee "White coffee"), and a locally manufactured beer called Al Shark.[[365]](#cite_note-376)
